title: AGENTS.md
text:
This repository is a Quarto website for an Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) course portfolio. The primary grading focus is the quality of your OOP design and maintainability, not visual polish.



_quarto.yml: Quarto website configuration (navbar, theme, CSS).
index.qmd: Home page.
projects/: Project pages and generated project listing.

projects/_generated_projects.qmd: Generated project grid (do not edit by hand).
projects/_generated_pinned_projects.qmd: Generated pinned projects block.

data/projects.csv: Source-of-truth project data.
assets/project_thumbs/: Project thumbnail images.
portfolio_content/: OO content pipeline package.

models.py: Domain model (Project).
csv_row_parser.py: CSV row parsing + field validation.
repository.py: CSV loading/filtering/sorting.
specs.py: Typed immutable render specs.
viewmodels.py: Project -> viewmodel mapping.
renderers.py: Quarto/HTML rendering.
text_utils.py: Shared formatting utilities.

scripts/build_site_content.py: Build orchestration via SiteContentBuilder.





Edit data in data/projects.csv.
Add thumbnails to assets/project_thumbs/.

Convention: thumbnail file name is the project slug:

assets/project_thumbs/<slug>.png (default)
If the CSV Thumbnail column contains a filename with a different extension (e.g. .jpg), the generator uses that extension for the slug file.

Therefore: you only need to ensure assets/project_thumbs/<slug>.<ext> exists.

Regenerate the partial:

python scripts/build_site_content.py

Preview the site (see “Local dev server” below).





Single Responsibility Principle (SRP): keep parsing, sorting, and rendering in separate classes/modules.
Typed domain model: Project is the central data model; avoid passing around raw dicts.
Deterministic output: the generator should always produce the same output given the same inputs (stable sorting).
No “stringly-typed” templates scattered across .qmd: Quarto pages should mostly include generated partials.




From repo root:
# (Optional) regenerate generated content first
python scripts/build_site_content.py

# Start Quarto live preview server
quarto preview
Quarto will print a local URL (typically http://localhost:xxxx) and auto-reload on changes.



From repo root:
# Regenerate generated content (partials + per-project pages)
python scripts/build_site_content.py

# Render site to _site/
quarto render

# Run unit tests for pipeline boundaries
pytest



Home: index.qmd → Featured Projects section is clean and not broken.
Projects listing: projects/index.qmd → featured card + tags panel + grid layout looks correct.
One project detail page: projects/<slug>.qmd (e.g. projects/vox-populi-us2024.qmd) → header renders, external link works, optional body include doesn’t error if missing.





The example Shiny app lives in projects/data/app.py.
pip install -r requirements.txt
cd projects/data
shiny run app.py



Preferred: publish with Quarto’s built-in GitHub Pages publishing.
# one-time setup may be required in GitHub repo settings
quarto publish gh-pages
This publishes the rendered site to the gh-pages branch and configures Pages to serve it.
If you prefer a manual flow, you can render locally and push the output branch, but avoid committing _site/ unless your chosen workflow explicitly requires it.

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text: An interactive web application built with Shiny for exploring and visualizing the Iris dataset. This app allows you to filter data by species and view correlations between sepal and petal measurements.

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text:
Preview
The Data Explorer app provides an interactive interface for analyzing the Iris dataset:



Iris Data Visualization


This visualization shows the relationship between sepal length and petal length, colored by species. The app allows you to filter by species and view detailed measurements for each data point.

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text:
Interactive Demo
Interact with the live Shiny application below:

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text:
Features

Species Filter: Select individual iris species or view all data
Interactive Visualization: Hover over data points to see detailed measurements
Data Table: View the filtered dataset in a formatted table
Dynamic Updates: All visualizations update in real-time based on your selection

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text:
Running the App Locally
To run this application on your machine:
# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Start the Shiny app
cd projects/data
shiny run app.py
Then visit http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.

title: Data Explorer - Iris Dataset
text:
Project Details
Technology Stack: - Python 3.9 - Shiny for Python - Pandas for data manipulation - Plotly for interactive visualizations
Repository: add links to notebooks and artifacts.

title: Mihály Hideg
text:
This project was built as an election-night “needle” for 444.hu during the 2021 opposition primary runoff, designed to answer one question in real time: who is likely to win by the end of the count. Instead of waiting for complete results, the display projected the probable final margin from partial returns and showed that estimate in a single, easy-to-read indicator. That framing made a fast-moving vote count understandable for a broad audience.
The original article text explains the model logic in plain language. The central value under the needle represented the currently expected end-of-day lead, while the colored ranges on both sides showed the still-plausible outcomes based on counted and uncounted areas. Importantly, the article notes that this interval could widen early and then narrow as more districts reported, which communicates uncertainty as a normal part of live forecasting rather than as an error.
As a communication product, the needle balances statistical projection with editorial clarity. Readers get a continuously updated estimate, but they also get a transparent explanation of what can still change and why. For election coverage, that combination is valuable: it reduces confusion in volatile moments and demonstrates how data visualization can improve public understanding of incomplete results.

title: Home
text:
About
I am a Data and Society undergraduate at CEU with a focus on election analysis, web development, and public-facing data visualization.
My current direction combines polling aggregation, voting behavior, and election-night nowcasting workflows.
Read the full profile on the About page.

title: About
text:
Introduction
I am a Data and Society undergraduate student at CEU, and I also work in web development with a strong interest in data visualization. My election-related projects include Vox Populi’s 2024 US polling aggregator and a vote advice application for the 2025 Romanian elections. My thesis will focus on low-coverage election-night estimation during the 2026 vote count, and I may also write about it on the Vox Populi blog.

title: About
text:
Current Work

Election-focused visualizations and polling aggregation products.
Web interfaces that make complex analysis accessible to non-technical audiences.
Reproducible content pipelines using Python and Quarto.

title: About
text:
Studies and Focus Areas

CEU - Data and Society (BA): quantitative social science, data analysis, and research design.
Thematic focus: voting behavior, mandate estimation, and polling quality.
Technical focus: Python, pandas, SQL, Quarto, and web data visualization.

title: Predictive election needle
text:
Predictive election needle
2021 • 2021-10-17
444.hu

An election needle that narrowed down the possible outcomes during the night of the opposition primaries.
444
data visualization
Open project ↗



This project was built as an election-night “needle” for 444.hu during the 2021 opposition primary runoff, designed to answer one question in real time: who is likely to win by the end of the count. Instead of waiting for complete results, the display projected the probable final margin from partial returns and showed that estimate in a single, easy-to-read indicator. That framing made a fast-moving vote count understandable for a broad audience.
The original article text explains the model logic in plain language. The central value under the needle represented the currently expected end-of-day lead, while the colored ranges on both sides showed the still-plausible outcomes based on counted and uncounted areas. Importantly, the article notes that this interval could widen early and then narrow as more districts reported, which communicates uncertainty as a normal part of live forecasting rather than as an error.
As a communication product, the needle balances statistical projection with editorial clarity. Readers get a continuously updated estimate, but they also get a transparent explanation of what can still change and why. For election coverage, that combination is valuable: it reduces confusion in volatile moments and demonstrates how data visualization can improve public understanding of incomplete results.

title: Mihály Hideg
text:
This project is a live election-tracking website for Hungary’s 2026 parliamentary race, centered on the head-to-head between Fidesz and Tisza. The landing view combines a “latest figures” panel with time-series charts, so visitors can immediately compare the most recent polling numbers with longer-term movement. A key strength is that the page does not only report point estimates: it also explains where the numbers come from and how they are grouped.
The methodology section is unusually transparent for a public-facing political dashboard. The site distinguishes between government-aligned and independent polling institutes, publishes links to raw polling data, and describes how weighted moving averages are used in different components. This makes the visualization more than a scoreboard: it is also a documented analytical pipeline that helps readers understand uncertainty and potential sources of bias.
Beyond vote-share trends, the website translates polling into projected parliamentary seats and presents those results in a second forecasting layer. It also breaks out smaller parties, provides embeddable/shareable modules, and links to longer methodological write-ups on the Vox Populi platform. Together, these elements turn the project into a practical decision-support tool for election observers who need both quick snapshots and deeper context.
